key_points: Energy enters ecosystems through producers and flows through different trophic levels
Only a small fraction of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next
Food webs provide stability to ecosystems by providing multiple feeding pathways
Biological magnification results from accumulation of toxic substances in food chains
Non-biodegradable substances remain in the environment for long periods
Improper waste disposal leads to environmental pollution
Human activities such as deforestation and pollution disturb ecosystem balance
examples: Pesticides entering crops can accumulate in animals through food chains
DDT concentration increases from plants to herbivores to carnivores
Plastic waste accumulates in soil and water because it is non-biodegradable
Decomposers like bacteria convert dead organic matter into simpler nutrients
Removal of predators can disturb ecological balance in ecosystems
formulas: Energy flow → Unidirectional flow of energy through trophic levels
Biodegradable substances → Materials decomposed by microorganisms
Non-biodegradable substances → Materials that cannot be easily decomposed
Biological magnification → Progressive increase in toxic substances at higher trophic levels
exam_tips: Understand how energy flows through ecosystems
Learn the causes and effects of biological magnification
Revise the difference between biodegradable and non-biodegradable substances
Focus on environmental problems caused by human activities
Practice explaining ecological balance and food web stability
